---
title: "GavinK_Comments"
output: html_document
---

Below contains some specific things I noticed while performing the code-fold audit. I have organized my thoughts by section *and only included sections where I noticed something*. I also added a section on **General Issues** that I have noticed.

In the General Issues section, I have marked issues that were discussed as ***DISCUSSED*** (and used my initials, should you also like to use this convention). Once an issue has been dealt with, we can comment it out.

See `reviewer-GavinK-CodeAudit.Rmd` for the code-fold audit itself.

MF: I'd like to treat this as a working document to be acted on for edits. On this pass will make "MF:" comments and assign Priority (L/M/H). It would be good to track things done as [**DONE**] or [**FIXED**] 

<!-- Commented this part out because we discussed it already:

One note: there were a number of places were I thought `ggplot2` could be formatted like it is elsewhere (i.e., \textcolor{red}{\textbf{ggplot2}}). I stopped listing these midway through chapter 4 as I was unsure if this was intentional and I was missing something. If this was not intentional, I am happy to edit instances of "ggplot2" for consistency. -->

# General Issues

- The inconsistency with "ggplot2" formatting (i.e., as `code` or `pkg("ggplot2")`)
  [MF: I made one pass on this, but perhaps some cases remain. Where is say something like: "ggplot thinking", OK as is. The canonical way to refer specifically to the ggplot2 package in text is: `r pkg("ggplot2")` so it appears colored red and generates an index entry. Priority: Low]

***GK: DISCUSSED***

- The inconsistency with example headings/divs (see `issues/example-headings.Rmd`)
  [MF: Noted. I'll look at these separately.]

***GK: DISCUSSED***

- When "data.frame" appears in-text, it is not consistently formatted as code throughout the book (i.e., as `data.frame`)
  [MF: This is context sensitive, depending on whether the idea of an R `data.frame` is explicit. Priority: Low]

- In the .pdf version, even-numbered pages have the chapter number twice (e.g., "*14 14. Multivariate Influence and Robust Estimation*")
  [MF: This could be be a Quarto bug, or something from my `latex/` files, trying to set the style of page headings. If not that, perhaps file this as a question on Quarto-dev. Priority: High]
  [FIXED: Revised `\chaptermark` in `preamble.tex`: root cause was `\markboth{\thechapter. ##1}{}` putting the chapter number into `\leftmark`, while `krantz.cls` `\even@head` already prepends `\thechapter` — producing the number twice. Fix: changed to `\markboth{##1}{}`. Verified on PDF build 2026-05-23.]

- In the .pdf version, some lines of code are long enough to exceed the boundary of the (blue) shaded box used to display chunks of code.
  [MF: These should be fixed by folding / re-indenting the code. Priority: High ]
  [ACTION 2026-05-24: Added `breaklines=true` to fancyvrb Highlighting environment in `latex/preamble.tex` — lines now wrap automatically at spaces rather than overflowing the box. Script `issues/wide-code.R` identifies remaining wide source-code lines (>65 chars) in PDF-visible chunks for manual attention if needed.]

- Capitalization of headings, subheadings, sub-subheadings, etc. should be made consistent
  [MF: My style has been Sentence case, except where an interior word following a ":" or a specific term should be initial cap. Would be good to go through these. Priority: Medium]

<!--
# Chapter 3: Getting Started

## 3.1 --- Why plot your data?

### 3.1.2 --- One lousy point can ruin your day

> In this example, it was arguable that x and y axes should be reversed, to determine how well measured weight can be predicted from reported weight. In ggplot this can easily be done by reversing the x and y aesthetics.

- `ggplot` can be changed to ggplot2 and formatted the same as it is elsewhere (e.g., bold + red).

MF: should be changed to: In `r pkg("ggplot2")` this can ...
-->

# Chapter 4: Plots of Multivariate Data

## 4.1 --- Bivariate summaries

<!--
### 4.1.1 --- Smoothers

> Non-parametric regression is a broad topic; see Fox (2016), Ch. 18 for a more general treatment including smoothing splines, and Wood (2006) for generalized additive models, fit using method = "gam" in ggplot2, which is the default when the largest group has more than 1,000 observations.

- 'ggplot2' here is bolded but not coloured red
-->

### 4.1.2 --- Stratifiers

<!--
> In ggplot2 thinking, grouping is accomplished simply by adding an aesthetic, such as color = rank.

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

MF: Ignore like these
-->

> The story in Figure 4.5 is again different. Faculty in applied disciplines on average earn about 10,000\$ more per year on average than their theoretical colleagues.

- 10,000\$ -> \$10,000

- "on average" appears twice

MF: Fix these

> For both groups, there is an approximately linear relation up to about 30–40 years, but the smoothed curves then diverge into the region where the data is thinner.

- Group `B: Applied` seems to plateau between 20-30 years

## 4.2 --- Data Ellipses

> 4.2 --- Data Ellipses

- "Ellipses" -> ellipses

### 4.2.1 --- Drawing data ellipses

***Label: fig-ellipses-coverage***

- The code for this figure is already folded; however, I am not sure if it should be. It is hard to glean what the text both directly before and after the figure is referring to without seeing the code.

MF: this is a case where the code is really long, and folding it in the HTML is the right thing to do, with the "Show the code" button. Not sure what to do about the PDF version here, where it does appear.

### 4.2.4 --- Handling nonlinearity: Plotting on a log scale

> 4.2.4 --- Handling nonlinearity: Plotting on a log scale

- Capitalization after the colon is inconsistent with other labels (e.g., "4.5 --- Simpson's paradox: marginal and conditional relationships")

MF: After a colon, capitalize these, e.g., "Marginal and conditional"

## 4.5 --- Simpson's paradox: marginal and conditional relationships

***Label: fig-peng-simpsons***

- This may be one of the figures Reviewer 1 was referring to as "too small"

MF: Three figs side-by-side do appear too small, but it is better to do them this way for direct comparison.

<!--
> The ggplot2 code for the panels in this figure are shown below. 

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

MF: This use of ggplot2 code is OK as is
-->

## 4.6 --- Multivariate normality and outliers

### 4.6.2 Penguin data

<!--
> It is a bit tricky to label points selectively in ggplot2 when the criterion for which points to label is complex, or involves variables outside the data frame.

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red
-->

***Code that defines*** `peng.mod`:

- Chunk unlabeled, formatting of code does not appear ideally in the HTML version.

- This is *not* the chunk in the "Looking Ahead" callout in 4.7 

## 4.7 --- Scatterplot matrices

<!--
>  (The default colors are somewhat garish, so I use scales::hue_pal() to mimic the discrete color scale used in ggplot2).

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

MF: ggplot2 OK here, but the `scales::hue_pal()` should be an `r func()` reference
-->

#### Looking Ahead

- `peng.lda` code has same formatting issues as the mentioned chunk containing `peng.mod` (see 4.6.2)

## 4.9 --- Generalized pairs plots

#### The species/sex/island penguin `pairs()` plot

***Label: fig-peng-mosaic***

- Species labels overlap. Perhaps fix by rotating labels?

## 4.14 --- Exercises

> 7. Other implementations of parallel coordinate plots in R include: MASS::parcoord(), GGally::ggparcoord() and PairViz::pcp(). The ggpcp version used here is the most general.

- ggpcp not coloured red like other packages

MF: If this is already not fixed, these should be `r func()` references

# Chapter 5: Dimension Reduction

## 5.2.2 --- Mathematics and geometry of PCA

> In this equation,

- Point #3 ("The column $\bf{v}_i$...") has formatting issues (see "_i\$")

## 5.2.5 --- Visualizing PCA scores and variable vectors

> The variable vectors (arrows) shown in Figure 5.11 have the following interpretations:

- See points 2 and 3 in the list under the above quoted text. Bullet points have formatting issues

## 5.3 --- Biplots

### 5.3.5 --- Supplementary variables

***Label: fig-crime-factominer***

- Font/figure is a little small

### 5.3.6 --- Example: Diabetes data

***Label: diabetes-scatter3d***

- Is there a way to format the labelling in this figure so that the text is more legible (e.g., reposition/recolour labels)?

## 5.4 --- Nonlinear dimension reduction

> The world of dimension reduction methods reflected by PCA is a simple and attractive one in which relationships among variable are at least approximately linear, ...

- "variable" -> "variables"

MF: Fix this

### 5.4.2 --- t-SNE

#### 5.4.2.1 --- Comparing solutions

> It is more interesting that the sizes and shapes of the group clusters change from one solution to the other. The normal group is most compact in the PCA solution, but becomes the least compact in t-SNE.

- Is a brief explanation as to why this occurs warranted? Or an explanation as to which solution is preferable?

# Chapter 6: Overview of Linear models

> Overview of Linear models

- "models" -> "Models"

MF: OK as is, if this is the section title

## 6.1 --- The General Linear Model

> The General Linear Model

- "The General Linear Model" -> "The general linear model"

MF: A case where all should be initial caps

### 6.1.1 --- Model formulas

> (In this code, coef(workers.mod2) |> t() |> t() is used to print the coefficients as a one-column matrix.)

- Wondering if this block of text can be placed before the preceding chunk of code as opposed to after (with parentheses removed).

> So a worker with zero years of experience can expect an income of \$29162 and this should increase by \$1119

> This says that income is predicted to be \$23,068 with no experience, increase initially by \$2295, but that yearly increase decreases by \$330.

- Commas needed in dollar amounts

MF: Fix this

# Chapter 7: Plots for Univariate Response Models

- `spida2` package was used but not listed under **Packages**

    - `spida2` used in "7.1.1 --- Example: Duncan's occupational prestige" in chunk labeled `duncan-wald`

MF: spida2 is a problem, since never released on CRAN
    
## 7.1 --- The "regression quartet"

### 7.1.1 --- Example: Duncan's occupational prestige

***Reviewer 2:***

> p. 184: It is unclear which package provides the confidenceEllipse() function.

- I think this is referring to the chunk labelled `fig-duncan-beta-diff`. To fix this, can we simply change `confidenceEllipse(...)` to `car::confidenceEllipse(...)`?

MF: Yes, use car::

## 7.4 --- Effect displays

### 7.4.1 --- Prestige data

***Label: fig-prestige-allEffects***

- *x*-axis ticks for `income` are overlapping substantially

MF: At first glance, I don't think it worthwhile to fix this

## 7.5 --- Outliers, leverage, and influence

### 7.5.1 --- The leverage-influence quartet

***Label: levdemo1***

- `y = rep(...)` has no `times` argument

> Outlier: (52, 60) a low leverage point, but an outlier (O) with a large residual

- In ***Label: levdemo2***, `O` has `y = 65` instead of `y = 60` as described in-text

MF: Requires further consideration

# Chapter 8: Topics in Linear Models

## 8.1 --- Ellipsoids in data space and $\bf{beta}$ space

### 8.1.2 --- Data ellipse and confidence ellipse

> Thus, controlling for Stress, the confidence interval for the slope for Coffee includes 0, ...

> In directions in data space where the slice of the data ellipse is wide—where we have more information about the relationship between Coffee and Stress, ...

- In above two passages, "Stress" and "Coffee" should be shown as `code`.

MF: Yes; fix these

# Chapter 9: Collinearity and Ridge Regression

## 9.1 --- What is collinearity?

### 9.1.2 --- Data space and $\beta$ space

***Label: fig-collin-data-beta***

- *y*-axis labels are cut off on bottom 3 facets

## 9.4 --- Collinearity biplots

***Label: fig-cars-collin-biplot***

- Some of the text labels are obscured (e.g., by points or lines)

## 9.6 --- Ridge regression

> In many cases, analytical or computationally intensive methods have been developed to choose an optimal value of the tuning parameter, for example using generalized cross validation, bootstrap methods.

- "generalized cross validation, bootstrap methods." -> "generalized cross validation, [or] bootstrap methods."

MF: Yes-- fix

# Chapter 10: Hotelling's $T^2$

## 10.8 --- What have we learned?

> As we saw with the banknote data, the average univariate F was 236, but the multivariate F reached 392 by exploiting the relationships among the six size measures.

- *F*s need italicization.

MF: Yes: whenever I use a t of F in the text, should appear as $t$ or $F$

# Appendix B --- R Code for Figures and Analyses

- Some of the labelling is inconsistent, e.g., '--' vs. ':' in the below:

> Davis data-- Models and plots
> Salaries data: scatterplots

MF: this is a consequence of how the list is generated from the R file titles, given in `title:` in the script headers. I'm willing to let these pass

- Davis-reg.R is also listed twice: once under Chapter 3 and once under Rcode

- Capitalization of first letter after emdash inconsistent, e.g.:

> levdemo.R --- Leverage and influence demo
> hatvalues-demo.R --- leverage and the data ellipse

MF: Fix the `hatvalues-demo.R` script. NB: The R code appendix, ./Rcode.qmd, is generated from `issues/make-rcode-appendix.R`, which needs to be re-run when such changes are made in th R/files.

# Chapter 11: Multivariate Linear Models

MF: Stopped here; finish this!

## 11.2 --- Fitting the model

### 11.2.1 --- Example: Dog food data

> By default, the factor formula is represented by three columns in the $\bf{X}$
 matrix that correspond to treatment contrasts, which are comparisons of the Old formula (a baseline level) with each of the others.

- Should formula here be formatted as code (i.e., `formula`)?

### 11.2.2 --- Sums of squares

***Label: fig-visualizing-SSP***

- This image appears a bit blurry (low-resolution). Is it possible to obtain (or recreate) a higher-resolution version? Happy to do this if you point me to the code used for each panel (if available).

> These numbers are the variances in the diagonal and covariance between start and amount in the off-diagonal. The sign is important: In $\text{SSP}_H$ the negative covariance reflects the fact that for these brands larger time to start eating is negatively related to the amount eaten. In $\text{SSP}_E$ the covariance is slightly positive.

- SSP_H and SSP_E should be bold here

## 11.3 --- Multivariate test statistics

### 11.3.1 --- Testing contrasts and linear hypotheses

> For example, the contrast “Ours vs. Theirs” estimated by formulac1 takes 1.375 less time to start eating and eats 10.88 more on average.

- Does the dataset provide units of time? I am assuming seconds, but cannot corroborate this using `?heplots::dogfood`. Also thinking that both numbers should be rounded to the same number of digits.

## 11.4 --- ANOVA -> MANOVA

***Label: fig-manova-diagram***

- This image appears a bit blurry (low-resolution). Is it possible to obtain (or recreate) a higher-resolution version? Happy to do this if you point me to the code used (if available).

> A multivariate analysis would show a highly difference among groups.

- highly -> large

> From a rough visual inspection, it seems that means differ on the math test $Y_1$, with the physics group out-performing the other two. On the reading test $Y_2$ however it might turn out that the three group means don't differ significantly in an ANOVA, but the Shakespeare and physics groups appear to outperform the normal curriculum group.

- Might be splitting hairs, but the use of "however" here should be changed.

    - A possible fix: "... the other two; however, on the reading test $Y_2$, it might turn out ..."

> Figure 11.4 illustrates a second important advantage of performing a multivariate analysis over separate ANOVAS:

- ANOVAS -> ANOVAs

***Label: fig-manova-response-dimensions***

> (The pattern in Figure 11.4 (right) is contrived for the sake of illustration; it does not reflect the data analyzed in the example below.)

- Is it possible to use a different example case so that any confusion may be avoided?

### 11.4.1 --- Example: Father parenting data

- Would it be useful/necessary to include `str()` output for the dataset?

### 11.4.3 --- Example: Adolescent mental health

> How does the association (correlation, \$R^2) of anxiety and depression vary with age?

- Missing end \$ around $R^2$

***Label: addhealth-linhyp3***

- There is code here that duplicates what was shown previously in an (unlabeled) chunk. I included the duplicate code below (I added an `eval=FALSE` tag for the purposes of this review document):

```{r eval=FALSE}
coef(AH.mlm) |> rownames()
```

## 11.5 --- Factorial MANOVA

### Example 11.1 Plastic film data

- Should this heading be formatted like the other examples previously appearing in the chapter? For instance, 11.2.1 --- Example: Dog food data

### Example 11.2 Does defendant physical attractiveness affect jury decisions?

- See Example 11.1. Also, should this be "Example *x*: Mock jury data" for consistency?

***Label: MJdata*** and ***Label: MJdata1***

- Would this be better formatted if a subset (including only the variables used in this chapter) is taken of `MockJury` and then passed to `str()`? I believe this would reduce the amount of code shown.

## 11.6 --- MRA -> MMRA

> Similarly, the the case of multivariate linear models, some consider it necessary to reject the multivariate null hypothesis for a predictor term ...

- "the the" -> "in the"

### 11.6.1 --- Example: NLSY data

> In this analysis the math and read scores are taken at the outcome variables

- "at" -> "as"

> From @fig-NLSY-density we see that math and reading scores are positively skewed, anti-social and hyperactivity have distributions highly concentrated in the lower scores.

- Need to add a word after comma or split into two sentences. A possible fix:

    - "... positively skewed. Likewise, ..."
    
***Label: NLSY-coef***

- The `coef()` call can likely be removed as the preceeding text posits `coef()` as familiar to the reader at this point. The main purpose of this chunk is introducing the `tidy()` call.

## 11.6.2 --- Example: School data

***Label: school-etasq***

- I think this chunk might be better placed before the paragraph directly preceding it (so, after the paragraph "Similarly `etasq()` for ... all the others." and before the paragraph "In spite of ... contribute very little.").

## 11.7 --- Model diagnostics for MLMs

> Such plots are produced by heplots::distPlot().

- Change to `heplots::distancePlot()`

## 11.8 --- ANCOVA -> MANCOVA

> To help interpret these effects, bivariate coefficient plots for the paired associate tasks are shown in Figure 11.22. (The coefficients for the group variable SES are on a different scale and so are omitted here.)

- Punctuation is a bit off here. A possible change:

    - "To help interpret these effects, bivariate coefficient plots for the paired associate tasks are shown in Figure 11.22 (The coefficients for the group variable SES are on a different scale and so are omitted here)."
    
## 11.8.1 --- Example: Paired-associate tasks and academic performance

***Label: fig-rohwer-mod1-coef***

- I am going back-and-forth on if this figure should have its code folded or not. `coefplot()` was introduced before but arguments `parm` and `variables` are displayed for the first time here.

> They are not equivalent in testing however, because the full model uses a combined pooled within-group error covariance, allows hypotheses about equality of slopes and intercepts to be tested directly and has greater power because it uses the total sample size. 

- Needs a couple of commas:

    - They are not equivalent in testing**,** however, because the full model uses a combined pooled within-group error covariance, allows hypotheses about equality of slopes and intercepts to be tested directly**,** and has greater power because it uses the total sample size. 

## 11.9 --- What have we learned?

> “Automatic” model selection procedures like stepwise regression, while seemingly attractive are dangerous in that can increase false positives or drop variables that should, on logical grounds, be included in the model.

- Needs a comma, a missing "they":

    - “Automatic” model selection procedures like stepwise regression, while seemingly attractive**,** are dangerous in that **they** can increase false positives or drop variables that should, on logical grounds, be included in the model.

# Chapter 12: Visualizing Multivariate Models

> CDA: For more than a few response variables, these result can be projected onto a lower-dimensional “canonical” space providing an even simpler description,

- CDA acronym is only defined later on in the chapter

- "result" -> "results"

## 12.9 --- Quantitative predictors: MMRA

> In this model, parent’s education and occupation and their visits to the schools were highly predictive of student’s outcomes but their counseling efforts and the number of teachers in the schools did not contribute much. However, the nature of these relationships was largely uninterpreted in that analysis.

- Comma before "but" needed

- "parent’s education and occupation and their visits ..." -> "parents' education, occupation, and their visits ..."

    - Removed first "and"

- "parent's" -> "parents'" or "parental"

- "students" -> "student" or "students'"

## 12.11 --- MANCOVA models

> HE plots for this model for the pairs (SAT, PPVT) and (SAT, Raven) is shown in @fig-rohwer-HE-mod1-pairs.

- "is" -> "are"

> To visualize this in an HE plot (@fig-rohwer-HE-mod2), I simplify, by showing the interaction terms collectively ...

- Comma before "by" should be removed

> We can readily see the difference in means for the two SES groups (Hi has greater scores on both variables) and it also appears that the slopes of the s and n predictor ellipses are shallower for the High than the Low group, indicating greater relation with the SAT score.

- Should "Hi" be changed to "the high SES group" or `Hi`? 

- Should "High" and "Low" have capitalization removed (or formatted as code, e.g., `High`)?

# Chapter 13: Visualizing Equality of Covariance Matrices

## 13.4 --- Box's $\mathcal{M}$ test

> In practice, there are various transformations of the value of $M$ to yield a ...

- $M$ -> $\mathcal{M}$

> ... approximation is often used; otherwise an $F$
 approximation is known to be more accurate.
 
- Comma needed after otherwise

## 13.5 --- Visualizing heterogeneity

> A larger goal of this chapter is to use this background as another illustration of multivariate thinking, here, for visualizing and testing the heterogeneity of covariance matrices in multivariate designs.

- Think this should be reworded to avoid ", here,". One possible solution:

    - A larger goal of this chapter is to use this background as another illustration of multivariate thinking. This section applies this to visualizing and testing the heterogeneity of covariance matrices in multivariate designs.

> particularly when a test concludes that differences exist, and one is left to wonder ``why?’’.

- Opening quotation marks are of different style than closing quotation marks

## 13.8 --- Other measures of heterogeneity

***Label: fig-peng-boxm-plots***

- "product" plot (top-left panel) has *x*-axis label cut off

# Chapter 14: Multivariate Influence and Robust Estimation

## 14.1 --- Multivariate influence

### 14.1.4 --- Leverage and residual components

- There's an unnamed chunk of R code that is formatted differently from the rest of the book. Was there a rationale behind this or should the chunk be formatted for consistency? I have included the chunk in question below:

````
H <- sapply(x$H, FUN)
Q <- sapply(x$Q, FUN)
L <- sapply(x$L, FUN)
R <- sapply(x$R, FUN)
````

## 14.2 --- The Mysterious Case 9

### 14.2.1 --- Cook's D

> ... this is referred to as a `type = “stres” plot.

- Missing closing ` after "stres"

## 14.4 --- Example: Penguin data

> the predictor `species` if of course discrete, ...

- "if" -> "is"

> For ease of interpretation, I use a little `dplyr` magic to label ...

- Should `dplyr` be `pkg("dplyr")`?

## 14.5 --- Robust Estimation

- Footnote \#3 ("The tradeoff between ...") has a couple of issues that I am not too sure how best to solve.

## 14.6 --- Example: Penguin data

> The largest differences are for the coefficients for `Chinstrap`, particularly ...

- Change to: "The largest differences are for the `Chinstrap` coefficients, particularly ..."

# Chapter 21: Discriminant analysis

- Should "analysis" be capitalized in the chapter title?

## 21.2 --- Theory

### Linear vs. quadratic discriminant analysis

- See footnote \#2:

> These are implemented in the package `mda` as `mda()` and `fda()`, ...

- Should `mda` be formatted as `pkg("mda")` instead (as opposed to being formatted as `code`)?

### Prior probabilities

> Bayesian approaches (e.g., EnisGeisser1974) have brought new perspectives
to discriminant analysis

- Is "EnisGeisser1974" a BibTeX error? See .pdf version

## 21.6 --- Visualizing classification in discriminant space

> Compared with the separate 2D views in data space (Figure 21.2), this single view accounts for 100% of the variance due to separation of the species groups. You can see that Betsy and Dave are rather close to the other Chinstraps. Abe, Chloe and Emma are rather far from the centers of the species they are classified as, yet they are closer to their groups than to any of the two others.

- Should this paragraph be moved to after the figure is displayed (as opposed to before)?

## 21.7 --- Visualizing prediction regions

### 21.7.1 --- Partition plots with `partimat()`

- See footnote \#5:

> All the Chinstraps appear mixed in with the Adélies and, giving an error rate of 0.204 in this plot.

- Remove "and" from the above

## 21.9 --- Relation to MANOVA and CDA

> In the table printed, the eigenvalues are...

- "In the table printed" -> "In the printed table"

# Case Studies

## Fitting the MLM

### Canonical space

> We can gain further insight, and a simplified plot showing all the response variables by projecting the MANOVA into the canonical space, which is entirely 2-dimensional ...

- Comma needed after "variables"

<!-- First pass (and code audit) of the book complete! -->
